title: Evaluating SFT Checkpoints
concept: Evaluate SFT models using automated benchmarks and human preference ratings to find the sweet spot of alignment. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Multi-Task Instruction Tuning.'

exercises.prompt: Why do we evaluate SFT checkpoints?

options.text: To make the model train faster
To find the best version that balances accuracy and helpfulness
To delete all the data we don't need

exercises.explain: Evaluating SFT checkpoints helps us find the best model version before releasing it, ensuring it is both accurate and safe.

cards.title: Is Your AI Ready?
cards.body: Imagine you've just trained your AI to follow instructions. How do you know if it's actually ready to talk to real users? You can't just hope for the best! We need to test its brain at different stages to find the absolute best version.

cards.title: SFT Evaluation
cards.body: Evaluating SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) Checkpoints means testing your trained AI model at different points in its training. We use automated test sets to check its factual accuracy and human reviewers to rate how helpful, polite, and natural its answers feel.

cards.title: The Soup Tasting
cards.body: Imagine SFT evaluation is like tasting a pot of soup. Automated benchmarks are like checking the soup's temperature with a thermometer—quick, precise, and objective. Human evaluation is like having a chef taste a spoonful—subjective, looking for the perfect blend of spices and balance that a machine simply cannot measure.

cards.title: Checking the Email AI
cards.body: Let's look at an SFT model trained to write emails. The automated test checks if the email has the correct recipient format and no typos. Meanwhile, a human evaluator reads the email and rates it 5 stars because it sounds genuinely friendly and professional, rather than robotic.

cards.title: The Perfect Score Myth
cards.body: Myth: A model with a perfect score on automated benchmarks is always the best helper. Reality: High test scores don't guarantee a good conversation. A model might get math questions right but sound incredibly rude or robotic to a human user. Both stats and human taste matter!

cards.title: Evaluation Balance
cards.body: We balance two types of tests to evaluate our SFT models effectively.

left.text: Fast, cheap, and tests factual accuracy or coding skills.

exercises.prompt: What is a major limitation of relying ONLY on automated benchmarks to evaluate SFT models?

options.text: They are much slower than hiring human reviewers.
They are extremely expensive to run on computer servers.
They struggle to judge subjective qualities like tone and helpfulness.
They can only test math equations.

exercises.explain: Automated tests are fast and cheap, but they cannot easily measure subjective qualities like tone, politeness, and general helpfulness. That's why we need human feedback.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using both automated benchmarks and human ratings is the best way to evaluate an SFT model.
exercises.explain: Automated benchmarks and human ratings test different things (accuracy vs. style). Combining them gives the most complete picture of a model's performance.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the evaluation strategy.
exercises.sentence: To test an SFT model, we use automated benchmarks to score accuracy, and human ratings to judge conversational quality.
exercises.explain: Automated benchmarks quickly calculate accuracy scores, while human judges evaluate the style, tone, and actual helpfulness of the responses.

exercises.prompt: Which of these reviews represents a human preference evaluation rather than an automated benchmark?

options.text: Reviewer A: 'The checkpoint scored 89% on the multiple-choice math dataset.'
Reviewer B: 'This model explains the steps politely, but it sounds a bit too repetitive at the end.'
Reviewer C: 'The model has 7 billion parameters and was saved at step 5000.'

exercises.explain: Reviewer B highlights the nuance of human judgment, focusing on conversational flow, tone, and helpfulness, which automated math benchmarks miss.

exercises.prompt: How do developers best evaluate an SFT checkpoint to ensure it is both accurate and aligned with human preferences?

options.text: By training the model on twice as much raw web data
By combining automated benchmarks with human preference ratings
By only letting other AI models grade the answers
By checking if the model's vocabulary size has increased

exercises.explain: You nailed it! Evaluating SFT models requires a careful mix of automated tests for speed and human ratings for quality. You are ready for the next level. Next up: Multi-Task Instruction Tuning.
